questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A binary tree node typically stores:? Choose the strongest option.
options: A value plus left and right child references (and optionally parent)
Only an array of unbounded children with no value
A hash table of all descendants
questions.explanation: Binary means at most two children; value + left/right is the standard shape.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: How does a Binary Search Tree (BST) constrain node ordering? Which response is most accurate?
options: Children are always greater than the parent
Left subtree values ≤ node ≤ right subtree values (per the chosen invariant)
No ordering—BST means “balanced search tree” only
questions.explanation: BST search property orders left/right relative to the node (duplicates policy varies).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Inorder traversal of a binary tree visits:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Level by level left to right
questions.explanation: Inorder = left → node → right; on a BST this yields sorted order.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Preorder traversal visits:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Preorder processes the node before its subtrees—useful for prefix/serialization sketches.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Postorder traversal visits:? Choose the strongest option.
options: In sorted order on any binary tree
questions.explanation: Postorder finishes children before the node—natural for delete/free and some expression trees.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Height of a tree is commonly defined as:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The number of nodes in the tree
Length of the longest root-to-leaf path (edges or nodes—be consistent with the problem)
The width of the bottom level only
questions.explanation: Height measures deepest path; confirm whether the site counts edges or nodes.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Depth of a node is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Always equal to the tree height
The number of children it has
Its distance from the root along the unique path
questions.explanation: Depth is root→node distance; height is often node→deepest leaf (definitions vary slightly by text).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A skewed binary tree (like a linked list) of n nodes has height:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Chain-shaped trees are linear height—BST ops degrade to O(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A perfectly balanced binary tree with n nodes has height:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Balanced binary trees keep height logarithmic in n.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Level-order traversal is implemented with:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: BFS with a queue emits nodes level by level.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Searching for a key in a balanced BST is typically:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Each step discards a side; height is O(log n) when balanced.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Inserting into a BST (standard, no rebalancing) places the new key:? Choose the strongest option.
options: In sorted array order without pointers
At a null child link found by BST search walk
questions.explanation: Walk like search until a null link; attach there—may unbalance over time.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Which structure is a tree but not necessarily binary? Which response is most accurate?
options: A file-system directory hierarchy with many children per folder
A singly linked list node with one next only—wait, lists aren’t trees with branching
A BST node with left/right only
questions.explanation: General trees allow arbitrary arity; binary restricts to two children.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: A leaf node is defined as:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Leaves have null/empty child links.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Full binary tree (common definition) means:? Choose the strongest option.
questions.explanation: Full: no one-child nodes. Complete/perfect are stronger level-filling notions.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Complete binary tree (heap shape) means:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Nodes may be scattered on the last level right-to-left
All levels full except possibly the last, which fills left to right
questions.explanation: Completeness enables array-backed heaps with parent/child index math.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why is a tree different from a general directed graph? Which response is most accurate?
options: A tree is an acyclic connected graph with one root path to each node (no cross edges in the rooted sense)
Trees always have exactly two nodes
questions.explanation: No cycles and a unique path from root to each node in a rooted tree.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Recursion is natural on trees because:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Subtrees are smaller instances of the same structure
questions.explanation: Divide-and-conquer on left/right (or children) mirrors the data shape.

questions.stem: In an interview you must state the height of a perfectly balanced binary tree with n nodes (order-of-magnitude). What is the right answer?
options: Θ(h) where h is unrelated to n
Θ(2h) because each level doubles height cost
Θ(log n) — balanced trees have logarithmic height
questions.explanation: A perfectly balanced binary tree has height Θ(log n); skewed trees can be Θ(n).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: BST vs hash table for ordered iteration of keys:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Hash table wins with O(n) sorted iteration always without extra work
BST cannot search in O(log n) when balanced
BST (or tree map) can iterate in sorted order via inorder; hash tables need sorting
questions.explanation: Ordered views are a tree/skip-list strength; hashes are unordered.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) of two nodes is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The deepest node that has both as descendants (including possibly one of them)
questions.explanation: LCA is the shared ancestor of greatest depth.

questions.stem: In a BST, LCA of two keys can often be found by walking from the root until:
options: You split—one key left, one key right (or hit one of the keys)
questions.explanation: While both keys are on one side, go that way; the fork (or match) is the LCA.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Serializing a binary tree for reconstruction typically must encode:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Only non-null values in preorder without null markers
Structure, e.g. preorder with null sentinels, or level-order with nulls
questions.explanation: You need enough information to place nulls; inorder alone is ambiguous without more.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Why can’t you rebuild a unique binary tree from inorder alone? Which response is most accurate?
options: Inorder is illegal on binary trees
Many shapes share the same inorder sequence
questions.explanation: Classic result: need another traversal (e.g. preorder+inorder) or explicit nulls.

questions.stem: Given preorder and inorder of a binary tree with unique values, you rebuild by:
options: Taking preorder’s first as root, splitting inorder into left/right ranges, recursing
questions.explanation: Preorder root + inorder partition is the standard reconstruction.

questions.stem: Validating a BST correctly requires more than “left < node < right” for children only because:
options: That local check is sufficient always
All nodes in the left subtree must respect upper/lower bounds, not just the child
Right children can be smaller if deep
questions.explanation: A grandchild can violate ancestor bounds if you only check immediate children.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Diameter of a binary tree (longest path) is often computed by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Only the height of the root
A DFS returning height while tracking max of leftHeight+rightHeight at each node
questions.explanation: The longest path may not pass through the root; update a global max during height DFS.

questions.stem: Checking if a tree is height-balanced (AVL-style |left−right|≤1 everywhere) uses:
options: Only comparing root’s two children heights
A postorder that returns height or a failure sentinel
questions.explanation: Every subtree must be balanced—bottom-up height checks.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Path sum problems (root to leaf equals target) typically:? Choose the strongest option.
options: DFS subtracting or accumulating along the path, success at a leaf
Sorting all root-to-leaf sums first in O(1)
questions.explanation: Explore paths with running sum; leaf check against target.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Morris traversal’s pitch is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: O(n) time with O(1) extra space via temporary threaded links
questions.explanation: Thread predecessors temporarily to avoid a stack; restore links as you go.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Kth smallest in a BST is efficiently found by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Sorting a hash set of all values after dumping
Inorder traversal counting until k (or augmented tree sizes)
questions.explanation: Inorder of BST is sorted; stop at k, or use subtree counts for O(h).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Deleting a BST node with two children usually:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Removes the root of the whole tree always
Converts the tree to a list first
Replaces with inorder successor or predecessor, then deletes that node
questions.explanation: Successor (min of right) preserves BST order when spliced in.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Flatten binary tree to linked list (preorder) in place often:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Uses right pointers as “next” after rearranging left into the right spine
Requires a new array of nodes only
Needs a queue of all leaves
questions.explanation: Classic problem: produce a right-skewed list following preorder.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Symmetric tree check compares:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Left subtree mirrored against right subtree
questions.explanation: Mirror equality: outer/inner pairs recursively or with a queue.

questions.stem: Maximum path sum (any node to any node) differs from root-to-leaf path sum because:
options: It may bend at a node using both left and right gains, and path need not include root
Negative values are ignored by definition
questions.explanation: Harder DP-on-tree: track best downward gain vs best path through node.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Trie (prefix tree) specializes trees for:? Choose the strongest option.
options: String prefixes—edges/characters along paths for autocomplete/search
questions.explanation: Tries share prefixes; not the same as BSTs on whole strings necessarily.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Segment trees / Fenwick are used when you need:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Range queries and updates faster than naive O(n) scans
Strict BST ordering of arbitrary objects
questions.explanation: These trees accelerate range aggregates—different purpose than BSTs.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Constructing a balanced BST from a sorted array is done by:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Inserting left-to-right sequentially into an empty BST
Picking mid as root recursively (left/right halves as subtrees)
questions.explanation: Mid-as-root yields logarithmic height from sorted unique keys.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Vertical order / column traversal often needs:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Assigning x-coordinates and grouping (BFS with column index is common)
questions.explanation: Track horizontal distance; BFS keeps upper nodes first within a column.

questions.stem: Time complexity of search/insert in an unbalanced BST built from sorted ascending inserts is:
options: O(n) — the tree skews into a list
questions.explanation: Sorted inserts without rotations produce a stick—ops become linear.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Senior pitfall: recursive tree DFS on adversarial depth causes:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Call-stack overflow; prefer iterative traversal or ensure balanced inputs/limits
Automatic AVL balancing by the JVM
questions.explanation: Skewed trees + recursion depth = production crashes; iterate or bound.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Claiming “BST search is always O(log n)” is wrong unless:? Choose the strongest option.
options: The tree’s height is logarithmic (balanced / self-balancing / random under assumptions)
questions.explanation: BST time tracks height; balance is an extra guarantee (AVL/Red-Black/Treap…).

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Parent pointers missing: LCA in a general binary tree still works via:? Which answer is best?
questions.explanation: Standard LCA recursion doesn’t need parent links; parent-climb needs them.

questions.stem: Serialization ambiguity: using only preorder values without nulls fails when:
options: The tree is a BST—preorder alone always rebuilds uniquely without rules
Different shapes can share the same preorder value sequence
questions.explanation: Null markers or a second traversal (plus uniqueness) are required for general binary trees.

questions.stem: Mutable shared subtrees (DAGs sold as “trees”) break algorithms that assume:
options: Each child link is an exclusive subtree—sharing causes double-counting / cycles of logic
questions.explanation: If nodes are shared, it’s a DAG; naive recursion may explode or mis-count.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: AVL vs Red-Black in interviews: a fair distinction is:? Choose the strongest option.
options: AVL is more strictly balanced (faster lookups, more rotations on update); RB favors cheaper updates
questions.explanation: Both guarantee O(log n); rotation frequency vs lookup height is the usual trade-talk.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Integer overflow when summing path values or heights sneaks in when:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Problem constraints allow large sums but code uses narrow ints without care
questions.explanation: Path-sum and similar problems often need long / overflow-aware types.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Thread-unsafe tree map mutated during iteration typically yields:? Choose the strongest option.
options: ConcurrentModificationException or silent corruption depending on language/structure
questions.explanation: Fail-fast iterators or data races—don’t mutate unsafely while traversing.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, you are asked: Using floating-point keys in a BST for “equality” is hazardous because:? Which answer is best?
options: Rounding/NaN make ordering and equality fragile—prefer exact types or careful tolerances outside BST invariants
Hash tables cannot store floats either
questions.explanation: Total orders and NaNs break search-tree assumptions; design keys carefully.

questions.stem: In a coding interview, how should you answer this: Production “delete tree” in C without GC should prefer:? Choose the strongest option.
options: Postorder free (children before parent) or iterative stack to avoid leaks and use-after-free
Preorder free of parent before children
Relying on the OS after exit only even for long-lived processes
questions.explanation: Free children first; postorder (or equivalent iterative) is the safe pattern.
